scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian is satirizing the specific judicial institutions of Athens, such as the Areopagus and the system of mass citizen juries.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The verbatim describes the classical Athenian jury system at its height; -350 represents the mid-4th century BCE when these democratic procedures were fully developed.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text explicitly mentions the taxation of 'naturalized foreigners' (metics) living near the Acropolis in the context of the Athenian service.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text refers to the era following the Persian Wars (Marathon) when the metic tax (metoikion) was a standard feature of the Athenian fiscal system.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The setting is the Areopagus, the historic court of Athens, describing Athenian litigant procedures.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the formal legal procedures and appeals within the Athenian court system of the 4th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the 'dikastikon' (juror's fee), a hallmark of the Athenian democratic legal system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The provision of pay for jurors was a key Cleisthenic and Periclean reform, reaching the cited 'three obols' level around 425 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The dialogue involves Epicurus and philosophical choices within the framework of a 'free citizen of a free state,' referring to Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Epicurus established his school in Athens around 306 BCE; the rule reflects the civil liberties of that era.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text refers to the specific administrative handling of juror fees in the Athenian court system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the mid-4th century BCE, the era of the Attic orators when jury pay was a central civic mechanism.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The Areopagus is the specific Athenian institution mentioned as lacking jurisdiction over certain 'outsiders'.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the 4th-century BCE Athenian legal distinction between citizen jurisdiction and cases involving foreigners.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The address 'men of Athens' and the reference to trial participation identify the Athenian demokratia.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This is the classic floruit of Athenian forensic oratory and citizen participation in courts.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Refers to Athenian citizen jury service and the associated state-provided fees.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Standard practice in 4th-century BCE Athens regarding the payment of double fees for multiple sessions.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Refers to the Athenian divorce court and the legal return of the 'proix' (dowry).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the 4th century BCE, corresponding to the family laws documented in the speeches of Isaeus and Demosthenes.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Relates to the Athenian practice of compensating citizens for attending court as jurors.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Standard democratic procedure in Athens during the mid-4th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Lucian is writing in the 2nd century CE; the reference to Phidias as a known historical 'sculptor' reflects contemporary Roman-era professional classifications.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian’s floruit (c. 165 CE), as the text discusses social recognition of professions in his own time.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes a general social expectation for both slaves and free men to possess a 'techne' (art) for profit, consistent with Roman economic life.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the author's floruit in the 2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The mention of slaves having 'arts' to be 'of use to themselves' reflects the Roman legal concept of peculium and skilled slave labor.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the author's contemporary observations of Roman social and economic structures.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The discussion of leisure and desire based on material wealth reflects the social stratification of the High Roman Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the author's floruit in the mid-2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Describes the standard (and often criticized) method of discipline in schools during the Roman period.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's own time in the 2nd century CE, where corporal punishment in education was common.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Refers to the frequency of holidays and working conditions for artisans in the Roman world.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to Lucian's floruit (c. 165 CE).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Refers to common didactic methods used by teachers in the Greco-Roman education system contemporary to the author.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the author's floruit in the 2nd century CE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim discusses Idomeneus and 'the rest of the soldiers' in the context of the Homeric poems/Iliad.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The text interprets social stratification and royal privileges specifically as described in the Homeric epics (c. 8th century BCE).

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: While discussing a general rule, the mention of 'philosophers and rhetoricians' being called up refers to the local duties of citizens in a Greek city-state under the Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the author's floruit, reflecting the compulsory civic/military duties of the Greek intellectual class in the 2nd century CE.
